summary: Verismo opera in four acts by Umberto Giordano (premiered 1896), set during the French Revolution. The title character is a poet who falls in love with the noblewoman Maddalena de Coigny; both are guillotined at the opera's end. Features the famous soprano aria 'La mamma morta' (used in the film Philadelphia) and the tenor aria 'Un dì all'azzurro spazio' (also called 'L'Improvviso').

works.name: Andrea Chénier – The Work as a Whole
works.description: Verismo opera by Umberto Giordano (1896), libretto by Luigi Illica. Set during the French Revolution. The title character is a real historical poet who falls in love with the noblewoman Maddalena de Coigny; the revolutionary Gerard (formerly a servant in her household) also loves her. Both Andrea and Maddalena go to the guillotine together at the end. Opens with Gerard adjusting furniture while his father sings 'Son sessant'anni' about the suffering of servants. The spy 'Incredibile' sees through the maid Bersi's disguise as a revolutionary. They meet at the Café Hottot. Was once staged on a floating stage on Lake Constance. The Maria Callas recording of 'La mamma morta' was used in the film Philadelphia. Wikipedia

works.name: "La mamma morta" (Maddalena's Aria)
works.description: Soprano aria sung by Maddalena in Act III. She describes the death of her mother in her burning home, and her subsequent journey through poverty, until love (personified) spoke to her. She offers her body to Gerard in an attempt to save Andrea's life. Contains the lines 'A dying body is my body / So take it. / I am already dead matter.' The Maria Callas recording was used in Jonathan Demme's 1993 film Philadelphia. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Sung by Maddalena; she laments her mother's death, then offers herself to Gerard to save Andrea; contains lines 'A dying body is my body / So take it'
examples: Maddalena agrees to give herself to Gerard in order to save the title character in this opera's most famous aria, 'La mamma morta'

works.name: "Un dì all'azzurro spazio" (L'Improvviso)
works.description: Tenor aria sung by Andrea Chénier in Act I. He describes a field of violets in a beautiful blue space and contrasts the beauty of nature with the poor treatment of the lower classes. Also known as 'L'Improvviso.' Opens with a solo cello playing a G# dotted half note followed by eighth notes B#-C#.

clues.clue: Tenor aria by Andrea in Act I; describes a field of violets in a beautiful blue space; contrasts nature's beauty with the suffering of the lower classes

works.name: "Nemico della Patria" (Gerard's Aria)
works.description: Baritone aria sung by Gerard in Act III, in which he questions his own motives after signing the denunciation of Andrea. A character questions the audience by stating 'An old fable that gladly the public still swallows' in response to questions posed in the aria.

clues.clue: Sung by Gerard; he questions his own motives ('An old fable that gladly the public still swallows'); aria title means 'Enemy of the Fatherland'

comprehensive_summary:
Andrea Chénier is a verismo opera in four acts by Umberto Giordano (1896), with a libretto by Luigi Illica. It is set during the French Revolution and is based loosely on the life of the real French poet André Chénier. The opera opens in the de Coigny household, where the young revolutionary servant Gerard (baritone) adjusts furniture while his elderly father sings 'Son sessant'anni' about a lifetime of servitude. At a party, the poet Andrea Chénier (tenor) improvises an aria contrasting the beauty of nature with the suffering of the lower classes — 'Un dì all'azzurro spazio,' also known as 'L'Improvviso,' which opens with a solo cello on G# before moving to B#-C# eighth notes. Maddalena de Coigny (soprano), a noblewoman, is moved by his words.

In Act II, the spy 'Incredibile' sees through the disguise of Maddalena's maid Bersi at the Café Hottot. In a Cyrano-esque moment, Maddalena reveals she has been sending anonymous love letters to Andrea, and the two pledge their love in the duet 'Ora soave.' In Act III, Gerard (now a powerful revolutionary official) signs a denunciation of Andrea before questioning his own motives in the baritone aria 'Nemico della Patria' ('Enemy of the Fatherland'). Maddalena then sings 'La mamma morta,' the opera's most celebrated aria, in which she describes the burning of her home, the death of her mother, and her years of poverty until love brought her back to life — and declares she is willing to give herself to Gerard to save Andrea. The aria contains the lines 'A dying body is my body / So take it. / I am already dead matter.' The Maria Callas recording of this aria was used in Jonathan Demme's 1993 film Philadelphia. Despite Gerard's regret, the Revolutionary Tribunal sentences Andrea to the guillotine. Maddalena insists on going to the scaffold with him. The opera ends with both characters being executed.

Andrea Chénier is frequently cited as a canonical example of verismo opera, alongside works by Mascagni, Leoncavallo, and Cilea. It was once memorably staged on a floating platform on Lake Constance.
